payload.description:
# End-to-end test infrastructure for execute_tactus

## Purpose

Layered test suite that validates correctness AND prevents regression of the safety / context-budget / debugability properties that justify this initiative.

## Test layers

1. **Service layer tests.** Existing pattern, extended for any newly-extracted service functions during the Epic 3 refactor. Pure Python unit tests, fast, no Tactus / no Lua / no MCP.

2. **PlexusModule facade tests.** New. A Lua-shaped harness that exercises each `plexus.*` method binding from the inside-Python side — verifies argument marshaling, return marshaling, error contract, context binding. Runs without an MCP transport.

3. **Tactus integration tests.** Small canonical Lua procedures that exercise:
   - require('plexus') resolution
   - basic ambient budget debit
   - sub-budget scoping
   - HITL-by-default trigger
   - sub-agent spawn with explicit budget
   - error propagation from Lua to Python and back

4. **Distributed dispatch tests.** End-to-end: `execute_tactus` invokes `plexus.evaluation.run`; remote workers receive budget, debit, stream cost events back, parent reconciles, runs to completion. Slower, requires real dispatch infrastructure.

5. **MCP transport tests.** Boot prompt + first-turn discovery against an actual MCP client. Verifies streaming, error responses, structured cost reporting.

6. **Token budget regression test.** Specifically: boot prompt size + typical first-turn discovery overhead must stay ≤5k tokens. Run on every change to boot prompt or `plexus.docs` content. Fails the build if exceeded.

7. **Cost accounting choke point assertion.** A test that walks the codebase and asserts no `litellm.completion`, `anthropic.messages.create`, etc. is called outside the Epic 4 accounting wrapper. Either via static analysis or runtime asserts in dev mode.

## Mock layer at the service boundary

The PlexusModule facade should run against in-memory implementations of the services for fast tests. Real backend reserved for a small set of integration tests. Local dev should require zero AWS credentials to run unit + facade tests.

## Distributed test infrastructure note

End-to-end dispatch tests are inherently flakier and slower than unit tests. Carve them into a separate test suite that runs in CI but not on every pre-commit. Local dev experience should not be blocked by the dispatch tests.

## Estimate

1.5 weeks, dominated by the distributed dispatch test infrastructure (end-to-end with real Celery workers requires careful test isolation).

## Dependencies

All other epics. This one is the safety net under everything else.
payload.title: End-to-end test infrastructure for execute_tactus
